text.title: Predictive Analytics in PM — Tools, Services, and References
text.summary: CLI tools, SaaS/OSS services, best practices checklist, and key references for ML-driven PM analytics.

list.item: pandas / polars — feature engineering, ETL of PM data (pip install pandas polars)
scikit-learn / xgboost / lightgbm — tabular ML for slip/overrun/risk (pip install scikit-learn xgboost lightgbm)
prophet / sktime / darts — time-series for budget runs and material prices (pip install prophet sktime)
shap / lime — model explanation; required for governance (pip install shap lime)
evidently / nannyml / whylogs — drift and data-quality monitoring (pip install evidently nannyml)
mlflow / wandb / dvc — experiment tracking and model registry (pip install mlflow dvc)
dbt — SQL transformations of PM warehouse data into feature tables (https://docs.getdbt.com)
duckdb — local OLAP on exported CSVs without a warehouse (pip install duckdb)
great-expectations — data-quality assertions in CI (https://greatexpectations.io)
prefect / dagster / airflow — orchestrate ETL → train → score → publish pipeline
Atlassian Intelligence / ClickUp AI / Monday AI — mostly LLM-summarization; minimal predictive ML; verify before integrating.
Power BI + Project for the Web — cheapest enterprise-grade visualization on Microsoft estate; REST-accessible.
Snowflake / BigQuery / Databricks — warehouse for unified PM data; required at scale.
Oracle Primavera + Risk Analysis — heavy predictive scheduling for capital projects (Monte Carlo simulation).
nPlan / ALICE Technologies — ML-driven schedule risk and AI scheduling for construction (REST API).
AlphaSense / Bloomberg / S&P Capital IQ — material price and macro forecasting feeds.
Anthropic / OpenAI / Vertex AI / Azure ML — LLM hosts for orchestration; route via privacy DPA + zero-retention only.

rule.statement: Calibrate probabilities (Platt / isotonic) before publishing risk scores — uncalibrated outputs produce false alarms.
rule.rationale: Raw sigmoid outputs from gradient boosters are not calibrated probabilities; stakeholders treating them as such will make misinformed decisions.

rule.statement: Version the data, not just the model — "model says X" is meaningless without "trained on snapshot Y".
rule.rationale: Without data versioning, debugging model degradation is impossible; DVC or warehouse snapshots satisfy this requirement.

rule.statement: Document model cards (intended use, training data, metrics, limitations) per Mitchell et al. — required by AI Act for high-risk systems.
rule.rationale: Model cards make assumptions and scope explicit, preventing misapplication and satisfying emerging regulatory requirements.

list.item: Hyndman & Athanasopoulos "Forecasting: Principles and Practice" — https://otexts.com/fpp3/
Flyvbjerg "Reference Class Forecasting" — https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Reference_class_forecasting
Mitchell et al. "Model Cards for Model Reporting" — https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.03993
Lundberg & Lee "A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions" (SHAP) — https://arxiv.org/abs/1705.07874
Sculley et al. "Hidden Technical Debt in Machine Learning Systems" — https://research.google/pubs/pub43146/
PMI "AI in Project Management" — https://www.pmi.org/learning/thought-leadership/ai-impact
Sibling methodologies: earned-value-management/, risk-management/, cost-estimation/, schedule-development/, pm-tool-selection/
